message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-004 in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
What: unfreeze() frozenset fallback for unorderable types sorts by stable (type name, repr) key (was hash-seed dependent).
Test(s): test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

91dfb1a fix(hardening-p4): batch B — concurrency, reasoning-tier & serialization (G-002,003,004)
82f58ca fix(hardening-p3): batch 5 — performance, with two scoped declines (5 findings)
1e3dce5 harden/pass-2: 33 production-readiness fixes (#124-#156) (#157)
452ad73 fix(audit-p3): close two latent _frozen.py contract gaps (convergence) (#28)
fc4a78b fix(audit-p2): resolve 6 adjudicated pass-2 convergence findings (#25)
0b0044d fix(audit-p1): resolve all 17 adjudicated pass-1 findings (#18)

1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def duck_mapping_items(value: Any) -> list[tuple[Any, Any]] | None:
62	    """Return ``(key, value)`` items if ``value`` is a dict-like NOT registered as a Mapping.
63	
64	    A lazy/proxy dict-like (exposes ``keys()`` + ``__getitem__`` but is not a
65	    :class:`collections.abc.Mapping`) would otherwise be treated as an opaque, hashable-by-
66	    identity leaf by both :func:`freeze` and the measurement↔interpretation fence — passing
67	    through as a live mutable object and escaping the fence scan (#140). Returns ``None`` for
68	    anything that is not such a duck-typed mapping (incl. real Mappings, str/bytes, sequences).
69	    """
70	    if isinstance(value, (str, bytes, bytearray, Mapping)):
71	        return None
72	    keys = getattr(value, "keys", None)
73	    getitem = getattr(value, "__getitem__", None)
74	    if callable(keys) and callable(getitem):
75	        try:
76	            return [(k, value[k]) for k in keys()]
77	        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — a misbehaving dict-like is not a usable mapping
78	            return None
79	    return None
80	
81	
82	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
83	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
84	
85	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
86	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
87	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
88	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
89	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
90	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
91	    """
92	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
93	        return value
94	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
95	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
96	    duck = duck_mapping_items(value)
97	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — freeze it like a mapping, not a leaf (#140)
98	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in duck})
99	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
100	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
101	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
102	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
103	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
104	        return bytes(value)
105	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
106	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
107	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
108	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
109	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
110	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
111	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
112	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
113	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
114	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
115	        )
116	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
117	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
118	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
119	    try:
120	        hash(value)
121	    except TypeError as exc:
122	        raise TypeError(
123	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
124	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
125	        ) from exc
126	    return value
127	
128	
129	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
130	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`.
131	
132	    Idempotent (pass 3, #F-034): an already-:class:`FrozenDict` value is returned unchanged so
133	    its lazily-cached hash survives. Re-wrapping (e.g. when ``ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__``
134	    freezes a payload that already holds FrozenDict values) would otherwise allocate a fresh
135	    FrozenDict with ``_hash=None`` and force an O(N·depth) re-hash on next use.
136	    """
137	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
138	        return value
139	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
140	
141	
142	def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
143	    """Recursively convert a frozen structure back into plain JSON-serializable containers.
144	
145	    The inverse of :func:`freeze` for serialization: :class:`FrozenDict` → ``dict``,
146	    ``frozenset`` → sorted ``list`` (stable order for reproducible output), ``tuple`` →
147	    ``list``. Leaves are returned unchanged. ``json.dumps`` does not know :class:`FrozenDict`
148	    (it is a Mapping, not a ``dict`` subclass), so an operator serializing a deep-frozen public
149	    payload — e.g. ``GateReview.payload`` — must thaw it first (#141).
150	    """
151	    if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
152	        return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
153	    if isinstance(value, frozenset):
154	        # sort for reproducible output; when elements are not mutually orderable (mixed types),
155	        # fall back to a STABLE key — (type name, repr) — rather than raw set iteration order, which
156	        # is hash-seed/insertion dependent and would make serialized output non-reproducible across
157	        # processes (pass 4, #G-004).
158	        try:
159	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
160	        except TypeError:
161	            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=lambda x: (type(x).__name__, repr(x)))]
162	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
163	        return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
164	    return value
165

content.text: Now let me see the diff for this fix and read the test.

content.content:
commit 91dfb1a4a4393317852caac2a5345cfc115348eb
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 23:52:20 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch B — concurrency, reasoning-tier & serialization (G-002,003,004)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed highs (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated):
    
    - G-002 (high) RESET lock_timeout on the bootstrap connection before it returns to the
      pool. The migration-lock bootstrap set lock_timeout at SESSION scope (autocommit, so
      is_local couldn't help); that connection was then reused for all later checkpoint ops,
      silently inheriting a non-default lock_timeout the operator never configured.
    - G-003 (high) BULK tier inner bound made robust + total. BULK relies on the HTTP client
      timeout (the sync-tier counterpart to HIGH_STAKES's anyio.fail_after) since a pool worker
      blocked in invoke() can't be interrupted by the outer _with_timeout. A bare float set every
      httpx phase to BULK_TIMEOUT, so connect+read could total ~2x and exceed the outer
      BULK_TIMEOUT+10 bound, leaking the slot. Now an explicit httpx.Timeout(read=BULK_TIMEOUT,
      connect<=10) keeps the total within the outer headroom.
    - G-004 (high) unfreeze() frozenset fallback for unorderable mixed types now sorts by a
      STABLE (type name, repr) key instead of raw set-iteration order (hash-seed dependent →
      non-reproducible serialized output across processes). Verified: the reverted version fails
      under PYTHONHASHSEED=7.
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 508 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
index ee92814..9f3ab7c 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py
@@ -151,11 +151,14 @@ def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
     if isinstance(value, (FrozenDict, Mapping)):
         return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
     if isinstance(value, frozenset):
-        # sort when elements are mutually orderable; otherwise keep insertion-free stable repr
+        # sort for reproducible output; when elements are not mutually orderable (mixed types),
+        # fall back to a STABLE key — (type name, repr) — rather than raw set iteration order, which
+        # is hash-seed/insertion dependent and would make serialized output non-reproducible across
+        # processes (pass 4, #G-004).
         try:
             return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value)]
         except TypeError:
-            return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
+            return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=lambda x: (type(x).__name__, repr(x)))]
     if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
         return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]
     return value
tests/test_immutability.py:43:def test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic():

content.text: The diff is clean and minimal. Now let me read the test.

content.content:
1	"""Frozen value records are deeply immutable AND hashable (audit pass 1, issue #7)."""
2	
3	import pytest
4	
5	from assay_engine._frozen import FrozenDict, freeze, freeze_mapping, unfreeze
6	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Unit
7	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
8	
9	
10	def test_frozendict_is_immutable_and_hashable():
11	    fd = FrozenDict({"a": 1, "b": 2})
12	    assert fd["a"] == 1 and len(fd) == 2
13	    assert hash(fd) == hash(FrozenDict({"b": 2, "a": 1}))  # order-independent
14	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
15	        fd["a"] = 9  # type: ignore[index]
16	
17	
18	def test_freeze_is_recursive():
19	    frozen = freeze({"x": {"y": [1, 2]}, "s": {3, 4}})
20	    assert isinstance(frozen["x"], FrozenDict)
21	    assert frozen["x"]["y"] == (1, 2)
22	    assert isinstance(frozen["s"], frozenset)
23	    assert hash(frozen) is not None  # fully hashable
24	
25	
26	def test_freeze_mapping_returns_frozendict():
27	    assert isinstance(freeze_mapping({"k": "v"}), FrozenDict)
28	
29	
30	def test_freeze_mapping_is_idempotent_and_preserves_cached_hash():
31	    # #F-034: re-freezing an already-FrozenDict must return the SAME object so its lazily-cached
32	    # hash survives — re-wrapping would allocate a fresh FrozenDict with _hash=None and force an
33	    # O(N*depth) re-hash. (ProvenanceEntry/DeterminismRecord re-freeze payloads that already hold
34	    # FrozenDict values.)
35	    fd = freeze_mapping({"a": 1, "nested": {"b": 2}})
36	    _ = hash(fd)  # populate the cache
37	    assert fd._hash is not None
38	    again = freeze_mapping(fd)
39	    assert again is fd  # same object, not a rebuilt copy
40	    assert again._hash is fd._hash  # cached hash preserved
41	
42	
43	def test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic():
44	    # #G-004: unfreeze sorts a frozenset for reproducible output; for unorderable mixed types it
45	    # must fall back to a STABLE key (type name, repr), NOT raw set iteration order (hash-seed
46	    # dependent → non-reproducible across processes). Pin the exact stable order so a revert to raw
47	    # iteration fails: by (type name, repr), ints precede strs → [1, 2, 'a', 'b'].
48	    assert unfreeze(frozenset({1, "a", 2, "b"})) == [1, 2, "a", "b"]
49	    assert unfreeze(frozenset({"b", 2, "a", 1})) == [1, 2, "a", "b"]
50	
51	
52	def test_verdict_is_hashable_and_evidence_immutable():
53	    v = Verdict("h", VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, "rule", evidence={"p_value": 0.01})
54	    assert hash(v) is not None  # no longer TypeError
55	    assert isinstance(v.evidence, FrozenDict)
56	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
57	        v.evidence["p_value"] = 0.99  # type: ignore[index]
58	
59	
60	def test_unit_attributes_cannot_be_mutated_in_place():
61	    u = Unit("a", attributes={"k": "v"})
62	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
63	        u.attributes["k"] = "x"  # type: ignore[index]
64	    assert hash(u) is not None
65	
66	
67	# ---- issue #19: freeze honors its hashable contract on leaves ----
68	
69	
70	def test_freeze_converts_bytearray_to_bytes():
71	    frozen = freeze({"b": bytearray(b"xy")})
72	    assert frozen["b"] == b"xy"
73	    assert hash(frozen) is not None
74	
75	
76	def test_freeze_canonicalizes_array_buffer():
77	    import array
78	
79	    frozen = freeze({"a": array.array("d", [1.0, 2.0])})
80	    assert hash(frozen) is not None  # array.array would otherwise be unhashable
81	
82	
83	def test_freeze_raises_loud_on_unhashable_unconvertible_leaf():
84	    class Unhashable:
85	        __hash__ = None  # type: ignore[assignment]
86	
87	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
88	        freeze({"x": Unhashable()})
89	
90

content.text:
Now let me analyze the test's discriminating power. The test asserts the exact order `[1, 2, "a", "b"]`. Let me verify this is what the fixed code produces and that the reverted code would fail. The key concern: would raw iteration order ever coincidentally produce `[1, 2, "a", "b"]`?

Let me verify both the fix produces the asserted order and that the reverted version fails under a hash seed.

"; done

content.text:
Now let me assess. The fix itself is correct, idiomatic, and seed-independent. The question is whether the test "discriminates" — fails if the fix were reverted.

Key finding: The test discriminates only probabilistically. Under the reverted code, it passes for some hash seeds (0, 1) and fails for others. Since the test does NOT pin PYTHONHASHSEED, and Python defaults to a random per-process seed, a single `pytest` invocation has a real chance of the reverted bug slipping through undetected.

Let me estimate the probability the reverted code passes (false-negative rate) empirically across many seeds.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
# Empirically sample the pass-rate of the REVERTED logic across many real process hash seeds
import subprocess, sys
prog = '''
def reverted(value):
    try:
        return [v for v in sorted(value)]
    except TypeError:
        return [v for v in value]
a = reverted(frozenset({1,\"a\",2,\"b\"}))
b = reverted(frozenset({\"b\",2,\"a\",1}))
ok = (a == [1,2,\"a\",\"b\"]) and (b == [1,2,\"a\",\"b\"])
print(1 if ok else 0)
'''
import os
passes = 0
N = 200
for s in range(N):
    env = dict(os.environ); env['PYTHONHASHSEED']=str(s)
    out = subprocess.run([sys.executable,'-c',prog],env=env,capture_output=True,text=True).stdout.strip()
    passes += int(out)
print(f'reverted code PASSES (test fails to catch bug) in {passes}/{N} = {passes/N:.0%} of seeds')
"
input.description: Estimate false-negative rate of test on reverted code

content.content: reverted code PASSES (test fails to catch bug) in 63/200 = 32% of seeds

content.text:
This quantifies the concern: on the reverted (buggy) code, the named regression test still **passes ~32% of the time** because PYTHONHASHSEED is unpinned and the test relies on a specific iteration order. That means roughly one in three CI runs would let a revert of this exact fix slip through green.

The commit message claims "Verified: the reverted version fails under PYTHONHASHSEED=7" — which is true for that single cherry-picked seed, but the test as committed does not pin that seed, so the discrimination is not guaranteed in the actual test environment.

Let me double check there's no session-level autouse fixture that pins the seed, which would change my conclusion.

content.text:
No conftest or CI pins the hash seed. The discrimination gap is real.

My assessment:

**The fix itself (the code change) is appropriate and complete** — it is correct, minimal, idiomatic, well-commented, and produces deterministic `[1,2,'a','b']` output across all hash seeds. It genuinely closes G-004.

**The named regression test does NOT reliably discriminate.** It asserts a hardcoded order but does not pin `PYTHONHASHSEED`, so against the reverted code it passes ~32% of the time (verified empirically: passes for seeds 0,1; fails for 2,3,7,999). The commit's "verified under PYTHONHASHSEED=7" only holds for that hand-picked seed, which the committed test does not actually enforce. A revert of this fix would land green in roughly 1 of 3 CI runs — exactly the failure mode this verification step exists to catch.

The fix is real, but the regression test under-guards it. Per the instructions ("the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted"), this is a CONCERN.

input.reasoning:
The code change in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (unfreeze, lines 153-161) is APPROPRIATE: correct, minimal, idiomatic, well-documented, and provably seed-independent. The TypeError fallback now sorts by (type(x).__name__, repr(x)), yielding deterministic [1, 2, 'a', 'b'] across PYTHONHASHSEED 0/1/2/3/7/42/100/999 (verified by running). It genuinely closes the hash-seed-dependent non-determinism of G-004. No regression: full restore verified clean (git checkout).

However, the named regression test does NOT reliably DISCRIMINATE. test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (tests/test_immutability.py:43-49) asserts the hardcoded order [1,2,'a','b'] but does not pin PYTHONHASHSEED, and no conftest/pyproject/CI workflow pins it either. I temporarily reverted the fix to the old `[unfreeze(v) for v in value]` (raw iteration order) and ran the ACTUAL test: it PASSES under seeds 0 and 1, but FAILS under seeds 2,3,7,999. An empirical sweep over 200 process seeds shows the reverted (buggy) code still passes the test ~32% of the time. Since CPython randomizes the hash seed per process by default, a revert of this exact fix would slip through a normal `pytest` run roughly 1 in 3 times.

The commit message claims "Verified: the reverted version fails under PYTHONHASHSEED=7" — true only for that cherry-picked seed, which the committed test does not enforce. To make the test a faithful guard it should pin the seed (e.g. monkeypatch.setenv + subprocess, or @pytest.mark.parametrize over several PYTHONHASHSEED values in a subprocess), or otherwise force a frozenset whose default iteration order reliably differs from the stable-key order regardless of seed. As written, the fix is real but the regression test under-guards it, so completeness (revert-discrimination) is not satisfied.
